topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official email landed in my inbox like a dropped stone in a still pond. Subject line: Important Notice Regarding [College Name]'s Future. My fingers hovered over the screen, tracing the familiar, comforting logo of the university. Every line of text swam before my eyes, a dizzying parade of administrative jargon leading to one unavoidable conclusion: the campus would be shuttered by the end of the semester. I reread the paragraph about facility closures three times, my breath catching in shallow gasps. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my dorm room, the cheap carpet feeling suddenly sticky beneath my bare feet. I kept glancing at the window, half-expecting to see the familiar quad, the bustling student life, but the view outside just seemed muted, distant. A knot tightened in my stomach, a cold, heavy pressure that made my chest feel tight. I needed to call someone, anyone, just to ground myself in something solid, but the silence in the room amplified the ticking of the clock on the wall, each second stretching into an eternity.

[story 2]

"So, they're actually shutting it down?" I managed to ask, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The words tasted like ash. I leaned against the cool cinderblock wall of the library, trying to anchor myself in the physical reality of the place, but the air felt thin, lacking its usual vibrant hum of activity. My hands were shaking slightly as I clutched the printed notification, the paper crinkling under the pressure. I looked around the cavernous reading room, at the empty desks where hundreds of students usually wrestled with textbooks. It felt like walking into a ghost town. A strange restlessness began to bubble up inside me; I couldn't sit still. I started walking, aimlessly down the main corridor, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead with an irritating, relentless sound. Each footfall echoed too loudly, a jarring counterpoint to the profound quiet that seemed to be settling over the institution. I kept checking my phone, a desperate need to see any sign of normalcy, any reassurance that this monumental shift wasn't just a bad dream.

[story 3]

I stared at the closing ceremony video on the university website, the bright, smiling faces of alumni flashing across the screen. It was all so cheerful, so forward-looking, and it made the present moment feel intensely wrong. A slow, creeping sense of dislocation washed over me, starting in my chest and spreading outward. I traced the outline of the campus map on my desk, my pencil moving in jerky, uncertain strokes. The sheer finality of it—the end of four years, the dismantling of everything I had built my future around—was overwhelming. I kept swallowing, the dryness in my throat making it difficult to take a full breath. I stood up, walked to the window overlooking the sprawling green, and watched the sunset paint the sky in hues of orange and deep violet. The beauty of the scene felt mocking, a lavish distraction from the stark reality settling in my gut. I felt profoundly adrift, like a ship without a compass, waiting for a direction that simply wasn't coming.
